message.reasoning_content:
We need to produce the literature_hvs_candidates.json for paper 2101.10878. The provided inputs include the skeleton, the catalog_review.json, catalog_extraction.json, the three ECSV files, and the full paper tex. The skill says to first read paper text, then use catalog files to map to tables. Need to determine which objects the paper treats as possibly unbound from the Milky Way / Galactic potential, i.e., HVS candidates.

Paper title: "Blue extreme disk-runaway stars with Gaia EDR3". It studies 30 stars, but only some are gravitationally unbound or possibly unbound. Need to check text.

From abstract: "They include HVS 5 and HVS 6, which are both gravitationally unbound to the Milky Way." Also, S5-HVS 1 is mentioned as most extreme, but abstract says "Only three stars in the sample have past trajectories that are consistent with an origin in the GC, most notably S5-HVS 1, which is the most extreme object in the sample by far." Abstract does not explicitly say S5-HVS 1 is unbound, but in text: S5-HVS 1 is clearly unbound (abstract says "it is also the only object that is gravitationally unbound to the Milky Way" in sect. something). Actually, abstract: "while most stars originate ... They include HVS 5 and HVS 6, which are both gravitationally unbound to the Milky Way." So HVS 5 and HVS 6 are unbound. Also, from the introduction and later sections: S5-HVS 1 is unbound. The text in Section 4.1 (subsection "Possible origin in the Galactic center") says: "Only three program stars have galactocentric radii ... may thus stem from the GC: S5-HVS 1, B576, and B598. These three are also among the top four with the highest ejection velocities, with S5-HVS 1 clearly standing out ... It is also the only object that is gravitationally unbound to the Milky Way." So yes, S5-HVS 1 is unbound relative to the Galaxy? Wait: "It is also the only object that is gravitationally unbound to the Milky Way." That refers to among those three? But HVS 5 is unbound, and HVS 6 is unbound. Actually, the sentence "It is also the only object that is gravitationally unbound to the Milky Way" appears in the context of the three stars that may stem from GC. It says: "Only three program stars have galactocentric radii at plane intersection that are smaller than 1 kpc and may thus stem from the GC: S5-HVS 1, B576, and B598. These three are also among the top four with the highest ejection velocities, with S5-HVS 1 clearly standing out ... It is also the only object that is gravitationally unbound to the Milky Way." This suggests that among those three, only S5-HVS 1 is unbound. But HVS 5 is unbound, as stated elsewhere. So the paper has at least S5-HVS 1, HVS 5, HVS 6 as unbound. Possibly also others as "possibly unbound" or "hyper-runaway" (but many are not unbound). Need to read section 4.2 and 4.3.

Section 4.1: "Only three program stars have galactocentric radii at plane intersection that are smaller than 1 kpc and may thus stem from the GC: S5-HVS 1, B576, and B598. ... S5-HVS 1 ... is also the only object that is gravitationally unbound to the Milky Way. ... the nature of these two stars is not yet clear ..." So S5-HVS 1 is unbound (clearly). Then in Section 4.2 "Origin in the Galactic disk": "HVS 5 is certainly one of the most interesting targets because it is, on the one hand, clearly gravitationally unbound to the Milky Way, and on the other hand ...". So HVS 5 is unbound. Also mentions LAMOST HVS 1 and LAMOST HVS 4 as "such so-called hyper-runaway stars" - but are they unbound? The term "hyper-runaway star" was coined by Przybilla+2008 for stars unbound but not from GC. But the paper's definition may include some that are bound. The text: "LAMOST HVS 1 and LAMOST HVS 4 are two further examples for such so-called hyper-runaway stars." However, later: "Interestingly, the object for which the hyper-runaway class was originally coined, HD 271791, no longer qualifies as member because it is clearly bound to the Milky Way. The same applies to HIP 60350. Whether LAMOST HVS 2, J0136+2425, and HVS 8 are hyper-runaway stars depends on the choice of the applied Galactic mass model." So some are hyper-runaway depending on the model. For our inclusion, we need the paper's own assessment of unbound status. Check Table 4 (kinematics) includes "P_b" (probability bound). For some stars, P_b = 0 % (unbound). For example, in ECSV Table kin, the col_012 is P_b. For star 1 (S5-HVS 1) P_b = 0, so unbound. Star 5 (HVS 5) P_b = 0. Star 6 (HVS 6) P_b = 0. Star 8 (LAMOST HVS 1) P_b = 2% (maybe bound? Actually P_b = 2, meaning probability bound 2%, so likely unbound, but not 0). Star 13 (LAMOST HVS 4) P_b = 0? Actually in ECSV line 54: P_b = 0 (line 704? The row for #13 (LAMOST HVS 4) has P_b = 0). So that suggests unbound. Star 10 (LAMOST HVS 2) P_b = 66 (maybe 66% bound), borderline. Star 14 (J0136+2425) P_b = 40 (40% bound). Star 18 (HVS 8) P_b = 55 (55% bound). So the paper classifies some as unbound or possibly unbound based on P_b. The paper itself says in text about HVS 5 and HVS 6 are gravitationally unbound. S5-HVS 1 is unbound. LAMOST HVS 1 and LAMOST HVS 4 are hyper-runaway, but does the paper explicitly state they are unbound? It says: "LAMOST HVS 1 and LAMOST HVS 4 are two further examples for such so-called hyper-runaway stars." The term hyper-runaway star originally meant unbound, but the paper may no longer consider them all unbound? However, from the text: "Whether LAMOST HVS 2, J0136+2425, and HVS 8 are hyper-runaway stars depends on the choice of the applied Galactic mass model." This implies that the others (LAMOST HVS 1 and LAMOST HVS 4) are considered hyper-runaway (likely unbound) irrespective? The earlier sentence: "the object for which the hyper-runaway class was originally coined, HD 271791, no longer qualifies as member because it is clearly bound." So they are classifying hyper-runaway as unbound stars from disk. So LAMOST HVS 1 and LAMOST HVS 4 are considered unbound. Moreover, in Table kin, for LAMOST HVS 1 (#8) P_b = 2 (maybe 2% bound), so essentially unbound. For LAMOST HVS 4 (#13) P_b = 0 (clearly unbound). So those two are candidate unbound. Also, there is mention in Section 4.3: "HVS 6 is unbound to the Milky Way while B481 and HVS 7 might be." HVS 7 has P_b = 42? Actually in ECSV line 53 for #12 (HVS 7?) Wait, HVS 7 is #12, P_b = 42, not unbound. The paper says "might be" meaning possibly, but not definitely unbound. So HVS 7 is not clearly unbound. So we have S5-HVS 1, HVS 5, HVS 6, LAMOST HVS 1, LAMOST HVS 4 as unbound stars. Possibly also B481? The text: "B481 and HVS 7 might be." So B481 might be unbound. B481 is #7, P_b = 60 (60% bound), so more bound than unbound. So not definitely. Similarly HVS 7 P_b=42. So not include. So the clearly unbound candidates are: S5-HVS 1, HVS 5, HVS 6, LAMOST HVS 1, LAMOST HVS 4. Also maybe LAMOST HVS 2? P_b=66, more bound. J0136+2425 P_b=40, more unbound but not clearly. HVS 8 P_b=55. So only those five are unbound with high confidence. But note that S5-HVS 1 is explicitly stated as gravitationally unbound in the text and P_b=0. HVS 5: "clearly gravitationally unbound". HVS 6: the abstract says HVS 5 and HVS 6 are both gravitationally unbound, and the main text (Sect 4.3) says "HVS 6 is unbound to the Milky Way". So yes.

Now, the paper also says "Only three stars ... S5-HVS 1, B576, and B598 ..." but those two are not unbound. So they are not included as unbound candidates. The target selection includes 30 program stars, but only a subset are presented as unbound. The paper also classifies some as "hyper-runaway" but some may be bound. So inclusion: only those that the paper explicitly says are gravitationally unbound to the Milky Way. So the five I listed. But need to verify if any other is explicitly described as unbound. In section 4.2: "LAMOST HVS 1 and LAMOST HVS 4 are two further examples for such so-called hyper-runaway stars. ... Whether LAMOST HVS 2, J0136+2425, and HVS 8 are hyper-runaway stars depends on the choice of the applied Galactic mass model." This suggests that LAMOST HVS 1 and LAMOST HVS 4 are indeed considered unbound in the current model (Model I of Irrgang+2013). Since they used that model, they are unbound. So include.

Thus candidates: S5-HVS 1 (cand-001), HVS 5 (cand-002), HVS 6 (cand-003), LAMOST HVS 1 (cand-004), LAMOST HVS 4 (cand-005). Need to map identifiers: for each, paper candidate number in Table 1: #1 = S5-HVS 1, #5 = HVS 5, #6 = HVS 6, #8 = LAMOST HVS 1, #13 = LAMOST HVS 4. Gaia source IDs from spectrophotometry table: for #1: Gaia EDR3 6513109241989477504; #5: 1069326945513133952; #6: 3867267443277880320; #8: 590511484409775360; #13: 1928660566125735680. So record those appropriately.

Now need to fill method_chain. The paper used: input catalog (Gaia EDR3), astrometric calibration (Gaia EDR3), spectrophotometric analysis (distance estimation, stellar parameter inference), radial velocity measurements (from spectra), solar position and motion (Schönrich+2010 values), Galactic potential model (Irrgang+2013 Model I), velocity calculation (transformation to Galactocentric, rest-frame velocity), orbit integration (backward tracing using Runge-Kutta-Fehlberg), escape/bound assessment (calculating escape velocity and P_b), origin assessment (ejection velocity, plane-crossing radius). So method steps: step-01: input catalog (Gaia EDR3), step-02: astrometric calibration (Gaia EDR3 proper motions and positional accuracy), step-03: sample selection (described in Sect 2 target selection), step-04: radial velocity measurement (from spectra, various instruments), step-05: stellar parameter inference (spectral analysis, Teff, log g, etc.), step-06: distance estimation (spectrophotometric distances), step-07: solar position and motion (adopt R0, z0, v_circ_sun, U, V, W), step-08: velocity calculation (combine distances, proper motions, RVs to compute Galactocentric positions and velocities, compute v_Grf, etc.), step-09: galactic potential model (Model I of Irrgang+2013, provides escape velocity), step-10: orbit integration (backward integration to plane crossing), step-11: escape or bound assessment (compute v_esc, P_b, v_Grf - v_esc), step-12: origin assessment (ejection velocity, r_p, flight time). But the skill requires steps to be separated: distance estimation, velocity calculation, etc. Also we need to include quality filters? Maybe step-03 sample selection is a quality filter. So step-03 for sample selection, step-04 for radial velocity measurement, etc. The chronological order: input catalog (Gaia EDR3), then the paper selects initial sample, then spectroscopic analysis yields stellar parameters and distances; but the kinematic analysis uses EDR3 proper motions directly, not derived from spectrophotometry. So the method steps linking to candidates: the astrometric data come from Gaia EDR3, spectroscopic analysis provides distances, radial velocities. So the distance estimation step depends on stellar parameter inference, which depends on spectroscopy and photometry. The solar position and motion is needed for velocity calculation. The velocity calculation uses observed phase space (positions from coordinates+distances, proper motions, RVs) and solar parameters to compute Galactocentric coordinates and velocities. Then that feeds into orbit integration, which uses potential model. Bound assessment uses escape velocity from potential model and velocities from orbit integration. So I'll create a DAG.

Define steps:
- step-01: input_catalog (Gaia EDR3 proper motions and positions)
- step-02: astrometric_calibration (Gaia EDR3 astrometry, proper motions) - actually input catalog covers that, but maybe separate? The skill says astrometric_calibration is a step type. But if paper just uses Gaia EDR3 astrometry as provided, that could be part of input catalog. However, the skill suggests separating input catalog and astrometric calibration. Since the paper does not recalibrate, the astrometric calibration is just using Gaia EDR3 values. So I'll combine as step-01 input_catalog, with outputs: RA, Dec, parallax, proper motions. Then step-02: sample_selection (cut based on ejection velocity etc., leaves 30 stars). That's not a per-candidate method, but part of selection. However, for the method chain of the paper, sample selection is important. So step-02 sample_selection.

Then spectroscopic analysis: step-03: stellar_parameter_inference (from spectra, model atmospheres to get Teff, log g, vsini). This also includes radial_velocity_measurement? Actually the paper measures radial velocities from spectra simultaneously. So step-03 could be radial_velocity_measurement? But the skill has separate types: radial_velocity_measurement, stellar_parameter_inference. Since they determined both from fitting spectra, we can have a step that does both. The paper describes "Atmospheric parameters ... were determined by fitting ... model spectra ... The resulting parameters were then used to compute ... angular diameter ... and interstellar extinction. Stellar masses M and ages tau_evol were inferred by comparing ... to evolutionary tracks ... . The spectrophotometric distance d finally followed ...". So there is a step for spectral analysis (including RV) and stellar parameter inference, then a step for distance estimation. However, distance estimation also involves fitting SED to photometry, so might include photometric_or_sed_modeling. The paper says: "a synthetic spectral energy distribution that was fit to measured magnitudes ... in order to derive the angular diameter Theta of the star and the interstellar extinction." So step-04: photometric_or_sed_modeling to get angular diameter and extinction. Then step-05: stellar_parameter_inference for mass, radius, age. Then step-06: distance_estimation from g = G M R^-2 and Theta = 2R/d. So that's the chain.

The kinematic part: step-07: solar_position_and_motion (adopt R0=8.3 kpc? The paper says "uncertainties in the distance of the Sun to the GC and in its relative motion to the local standard of rest (Schönrich et al. 2010) are treated analogously." The text does not explicitly state the values, but they probably used standard values like R0=8.3 kpc, z0=...? Need to find from the paper text. In Sect 4 (kinematics), they mention using Milky Way mass model I, but not explicit solar motion. However, Table 4 footnote? The table footnotes mention "The given uncertainties ... " and mention Monte Carlo and solar parameters from Schönrich+2010. The text in the paragraph: "Uncertainties in the distance of the Sun to the GC and in its relative motion to the local standard of rest (Schönrich et al. 2010) are treated analogously." So they used the values from that paper. I can look up typical values: R0=8.3 kpc, U=11.1 km/s, V=12.24 km/s, W=7.25 km/s? But the paper didn't state them explicitly. The skill says: "Record the paper's stated values (R0, z0, circular velocity at the Sun, solar peculiar motion U/V/W) as structured parameters[] entries with raw_value, cleaned value, unit, and source_refs. If the paper does not state them, keep the step and say in summary that the solar parameters are not reported; do not guess values." So I'll need to check if paper states them. Possibly in the text? In the paragraph after "To this end, we used the Milky Way mass model I ..., which is a three-component Galactic potential... The model reproduces ..." It doesn't explicitly give solar parameters. They might be included in the model I but not explicitly listed. So I should note that the solar parameters are adopted from Schönrich+2010 but actual numbers not given. So in solar_position_and_motion step, I'll put parameters like R0, z0, v_circ_sun, U, V, W with raw_value something like "adopted from Schönrich+2010" and value unknown, description "not reported". The skill says "If the paper does not state them, keep the step and say in summary that the solar parameters are not reported; do not guess values." So I'll set value as empty or "not_reported"? The schema for MethodParameterRecord requires raw_value and value. I can set raw_value to "Schönrich et al. (2010)" and value to "not_reported". However, the step summary should mention that values are not reported. That's fine.

Similarly, galactic_potential_model step (step-09): model I of Irrgang+2013, parameters: potential_name = "Milky Way mass model I of Irrgang et al. (2013)". The escape velocity definition likely from that model's escape velocity curve. So record that.

Step-10: orbit_integration (backward integration using Runge-Kutta-Fehlberg method, Monte Carlo). Step-11: escape_or_bound_assessment (computing escape velocity, bound probability P_b, v_Grf - v_esc). Step-12: origin_assessment (ejection velocity v_ej, r_p, flight time).

So the overall method_chain with dependencies:
- step-01: Gaia EDR3 input catalog, depends_on []
- step-02: sample_selection, depends_on [step-01, step-06?] Actually sample selection used proper motions from Gaia EDR3 and distances/parameters, so depends on step-01 (astrometry) and step-06 (distance). But sample selection occurs before the full kinematic analysis for the final sample, but it used preliminary distances and proper motions. In text they say "Based on proper motions from Gaia EDR3, we then carried out spectroscopic and kinematic analyses ... to filter out those with both a high ejection velocity and a relatively well-constrained origin within the Galactic disk." So sample selection uses kinematic analysis (velocity, ejection velocity) computed from proper motions and distances. So it depends on velocity calculation, distance, etc. It's a bit circular, but they used a first pass with DR2? Actually they used EDR3 to compute ejection velocities, then cut. So step-02 depends on step-01, step-04, step-05, step-06, step-07, step-08, step-09? Essentially the full chain before final escape assessment? But for method chain, we can simplify: step-01 input, step-03 radial_velocity_measurement (spectroscopy), step-04 photometric_or_sed_modeling (SED fitting), step-05 stellar_parameter_inference (mass, radius), step-06 distance_estimation, step-07 solar_position_and_motion, step-08 velocity_calculation (combine all to get Galactocentric velocities, v_Grf), step-09 galactic_potential_model, step-10 orbit_integration, step-11 escape_or_bound_assessment, step-12 origin_assessment. The sample selection step (step-02) would depend on step-11 and step-12? That seems odd. Actually, the sample selection is part of the overall method, but the paper first describes target selection as a separate block. The final 30 stars were selected based on the initial analysis. So I'll include step-02 sample_selection with depends_on [step-01, step-03, step-04, step-05, step-06, step-07, step-08, step-09, step-10, step-11]? That's messy. Possibly I can define step-02 as sample construction that uses initial criteria (Gaia EDR3 proper motions, prior distances) and then later the kinematic analysis refined. But the skill expects a DAG of steps that are atomic. I'll follow the paper's described order: target selection described before the analysis. That selection used prior knowledge of stars and some kinematic analysis? The text says: "Based on proper motions from Gaia EDR3, we then carried out spectroscopic and kinematic analyses ... of all members of this initial sample to filter out those with both a high ejection velocity and a relatively well-constrained origin within the Galactic disk." So they started with an initial sample of stars from previous surveys, then performed analyses. So the target selection (final sample) is after the spectroscopic and kinematic analyses. So I can define step-02 as sample_selection that depends on the kinematic analysis steps, but that would be after step-11. But then step-02 is not in linear order. However, the candidate stars are selected from this final sample, and then their properties are reported. The method chain for each candidate includes the steps that led to the reported quantities. The sample selection is not per-candidate, but rather a step of the paper. The skill says method_chain records the paper-level atomic method steps. So we can include sample_selection at the end or at the beginning? I'll put step-02 after step-11, as the final cut that produced the 30 program stars. That makes the method chain: step-01, step-03, step-04, step-05, step-06, step-07, step-08, step-09, step-10, step-11, step-02? But sample_selection filters the stars, and then the reported values (like ejection velocity, bound probabilities) are from the same Monte Carlo runs. So actually the values for the 30 stars are from the same computations, so the method chain for candidate extraction still includes all steps for each candidate. So I'll include sample_selection as step-02 before the chain, but it depends on results of the kinematic analysis, which is circular. Better to treat sample_selection as an independent step that uses preliminary runs, not part of the final reported quantity chain. However, the paper describes that they computed kinematics for the initial sample, then selected 30 stars. Then they present results for those 30, which are presumably the same computations. So the selection criterion was applied after computing ejection velocities and plane-crossing radii. So the method chain for the 30 stars includes the full computation. So step-02 could be placed after step-12, but that would mean the method for computing ejection velocities precedes sample selection, but the ejection velocities we extract are from the selected sample. That is acceptable: step-01 input, step-03 spectroscopy, step-04 SED, step-05 stellar parameters, step-06 distance, step-07 solar params, step-08 velocity calc, step-09 potential, step-10 orbit, step-11 escape assessment, step-12 origin assessment. Then step-13 sample_selection (cut based on v_ej, r_p) to produce the 30 stars. That's fine. Then in method_refs for quantities, we reference the step that directly produced them, e.g., v_ej from step-12, v_Grf from step-08, P_b from step-11, etc.

Now for candidate entries, we need to fill core observed_phase_space, derived_kinematics, bound_assessment, photometry, spectroscopy, stellar_parameters, orbit, astrophysical_origin, etc. Need to map from ECSV and paper text.

For each candidate, we'll extract:
- RA/Dec not directly given in tables; the coordinates are not in the kinematic table or spectrophotometric table, only Gaia source IDs. However, the paper likely does not list RA/Dec. The table of spectrophotometry gives Gaia EDR3 identifiers, but not coordinates. The paper may not provide coordinates. We can mention that RA/Dec are not reported. However, the observed_phase_space requires ra and dec fields, which can be null or we can note they were not provided. We'll set null and in inclusion_assessment mention missing. But the skill says for RA/Dec, if the paper does not provide them, they should be null. So leave ra and dec absent (null). That's fine.

- Distance: from spectrophotometry table col_012 (d) for the star. For #1: 8.63^{+0.31}_{-0.30} kpc, raw_value "8.63^{+0.31}_{-0.30}" (from ECSV line 31). Need to extract into QuantityRecord: raw_value "8.63^{+0.31}_{-0.30}", value "8.63", lower_error "0.30", upper_error "0.31", unit "kpc". Similarly for others.

- Parallax: not provided, probably not reported. So null.

- Proper motions: not in tables, not given. So null.

- Radial velocity: from spectrophotometry table col_006 (#1: 1017.0^{+2.7}_{-2.7} km/s). So we can fill radial_velocity with that. Note unit km/s. So for #1, raw_value "1017.0^{+2.7}_{-2.7}", value "1017.0", error? Actually it's +2.7 -2.7 symmetric, so error "2.7"? Or lower_error/upper_error? For symmetric, we can split as error "2.7" (positive direction) and also lower_error "2.7". But since it's symmetric, we can set error "2.7", and leave lower/upper empty. The schema: error for symmetric, lower/upper for asymmetric. So value "1017.0", error "2.7". Unit "km/s". source_refs from ECSV. method_refs: radial_velocity_measurement step (step-03). For #1, the spectrograph is "A (1300, 10 000)" from col_007, but that's fine.

- For some stars, radial_velocity has symmetric error, others like some have asymmetric? Check #5: 541.5^{+6.4}_{-6.1} (asymmetric). So split lower_error "6.1", upper_error "6.4". #6: 606.3^{+6.5}_{-6.2} asymmetric. #8: 612.2^{+5.7}_{-5.7} symmetric? Actually +5.7 -5.7 symmetric, so error "5.7". #13: 332.9^{+4.5}_{-5.0} asymmetric.

- Coordinates: need to note that RA/Dec are not reported. The inclusion_assessment could mention that coordinates are not given, but since they are not required, we leave null.

- Derived kinematics: from kinematics table. We have galactocentric positions x,y,z,r and velocities v_x,v_y,v_z,v_Grf, and at plane-crossing quantities. Also v_ej, etc. We'll fill galactocentric_x, y, z, radius, v_x, v_y, v_z, total_velocity (v_Grf), galactocentric_tangential_velocity? Not given explicitly, but tangential velocity can be derived? The paper doesn't list it. So leave null. We'll fill galactocentric_x etc from ECSV. For each star, the values are in table rows. For #1: line 30 in kin.ecsv: x = -4.12, y = -1.78, z = -7.25, r = 8.52, v_x = -720, v_y = -353, v_z = -1482, v_Grf = 1680. Errors are in next line. So we'll fill with raw_value from ECSV, value cleaned, error asym. For example galactocentric_x: raw_value "-4.12", value "-4.12", from ECSV cell, then lower_error from error line: "^+0.17_-0.15" meaning upper_error 0.17, lower_error 0.15. So upper_error "0.17", lower_error "0.15". Use that for asymmetric. For semi-symmetric, paper uses +/- notation. We'll map correctly.

- Bound assessment: escape_velocity not directly given, but v_esc can be derived from v_Grf - (v_Grf - v_esc) maybe? Actually kin table includes column "\varv_Grf-\varv_esc" in km/s. So for #1: 1080. We have v_Grf=1680, so v_esc = 1680-1080 = 600 km/s. But not directly given. However, we can include escape_velocity as a derived quantity? The paper doesn't list escape velocity separately, but we could compute it. The skill says "Do not normalize units by recomputing values unless the paper explicitly gives the converted value." The escape velocity is not explicitly listed, only the difference. So we cannot put escape velocity; we can note that it's not reported. For bound assessment, we have bound_probability P_b (in % unit). #1 P_b=0 (row 30 col_012 = 0). So bound_probability raw "0", value "0.0", unit empty (since probability 0-1, and it's 0%). Actually P_b is given as percentage? Table header says P_b (%) in the kin table description? The column header in LaTeX: "P_b | (%)" (see line 678: \# & $x$ & ... $P_{\mathrm{b}}$ ... and line 677 table has no unit in the header but from catalog_review column 12 name "P_b" but no unit text. In the LaTeX source, the header row includes "P_{\mathrm{b}}$ & ..." and earlier on line 677: $\varv_{\mathrm{Grf}}-\varv_{\mathrm{esc}}$ & $P_{\mathrm{b}}$ & ... without (%) but the tablefoot says "The probability $P_{\mathrm{b}}$ is the fraction of Monte Carlo runs for which the star is bound to the Milky Way." So it's a fraction, not percentage. P_b values in table: 0 for #1, #5, #6, #13; 2 for #8; 100 for many others. So raw_value "0", value "0", unit empty. But we need unbound_probability = 1 - P_b? The schema has both bound_probability and unbound_probability. For #1, bound 0, unbound 1. We can set unbound_probability value "1.0". But careful: the paper gives bound probability directly; we can fill unbound_probability as 1 - P_b. But the skill says for bound_assessment.bound_probability and unbound_probability, normalize value to 0-1 fraction. So for #1, bound=0, unbound=1. I'll fill both. For #8, P_b=2 => bound 0.02, unbound 0.98. But is P_b as fraction? The table shows "2" for #8, likely 2%? Actually the column header might have (%)? In the catalog_review, column 12 name "P_b" and no unit, but the table in LaTeX source: the header row includes "$P_{\mathrm{b}}$ & " without (%) in that row, but the preceding row in the LaTeX table (line 678) has " \# & $x$ & $y$ & $z$ & $r$ & & $\varv_x$ & $\varv_y$ & $\varv_z$ & $\varv_{\mathrm{Grf}}$ & $\varv_{\mathrm{Grf}}-\varv_{\mathrm{esc}}$ & $P_{\mathrm{b}}$& ...".
